notes: All four evals ask the agent to explain parts of the agentfiles repo itself. Reference answers are authored from first-hand inspection of the tree so grading is against ground truth, not opinion.

evals.name: architecture-layers-af-cli
evals.prompt: I'm new to the agentfiles repository at /home/eastill/projects/agentfiles. Walk me through the architecture of the `af` Python CLI tool (located under tools/python/src/af/). Identify the layers, name each one, list which files implement each layer, and describe what each layer receives and returns. I need enough of a mental model to know where a new subcommand would live and what it would wire up. Do NOT just list every file — I want layers, not an inventory.
evals.reference_answer: The `af` CLI is a Typer-based command-dispatch tool with five layers. (1) ENTRY POINT — main.py. Holds the `_SUBCOMMANDS` tuple list mapping subcommand name → module name, plus a custom `_run()` dispatcher that intercepts `install` and `research` (both use argparse/click rather than the Typer sub-app pattern) before delegating to Typer's `app()`. Receives argv; returns an exit code. (2) SUBCOMMAND MODULES — one module per command under tools/python/src/af/ (context.py, status.py, init.py, install.py, audit.py, plan_exec_cli.py, hub.py, mode.py, learn.py, log.py, note.py, read.py, research.py, index.py, metrics.py, preview.py, agents.py, etc.). Each exposes a module-level `app = typer.Typer(...)` with either a callback or commands; `_register()` in main.py imports the module and attaches its Typer app under the chosen name via `app.add_typer(mod.app, name=...)`. Each command receives parsed Typer arguments and performs its own work. (3) DOMAIN / CORE LOGIC — the modules that encode real behavior rather than CLI wiring: plan_exec.py (YAML loader + Plan dataclasses + DAG validator + toposort + StateFile), includes.py (fragment expansion for SKILL.md includes), archetype.py, caveman.py (compression rules), agent_knowledge.py, skill_find.py (keyword search over SKILL frontmatter). These take structured inputs (Path, Plan, strings) and return domain objects or error lists. (4) INFRASTRUCTURE / I/O — lib.py (bus path, ensure_bus, git_root), repo.py (git helpers), log.py, and data/ for static assets. Called by domain modules whenever they touch the filesystem or shell out. (5) INSTALLER — install.py is special: it's a click-based CLI (not Typer), invoked by main.py's `_run()` via the passthrough intercept. It parses manifest.toml (custom TOML-lite parser), discovers every `SKILL.md` under `agentfiles/`, and installs skills/hooks/CLI tools into `~/.claude/`. Cross-cutting: the hooks under /hooks/ (skill-logger.py, safety-gate.py, modes.py, notify.py) consume events emitted by Claude Code; they import hook_types from the same directory, not from the `af` package. To add a new `af <foo>` subcommand: (a) create tools/python/src/af/foo.py exposing `app = typer.Typer()`; (b) add `("foo", "foo")` to `_SUBCOMMANDS` in main.py so `_register()` wires it up; (c) if it needs to be installed as a skill/tool, add an entry to manifest.toml under `[skills.foo]` or `[cli.foo]`; (d) write tests under tools/python/tests/. Execution flow for any command: argv → `_run()` → (intercept install/research OR Typer `app()`) → subcommand module's callback/command → domain module → lib.py/repo.py I/O → exit code.

evals.name: trace-plan-exec-path
evals.prompt: In the agentfiles repo at /home/eastill/projects/agentfiles, trace the end-to-end execution path of running a plan through the plan-executor. Start from the user-facing CLI invocation (`af plan-exec <path>` or similar) and follow the call chain all the way to where individual plan steps are dispatched to skills. I want a numbered sequence, naming the file and function at each step, noting where data is transformed or validated, and where external calls (filesystem, git, subprocess) happen. Also flag any places where the path forks (e.g., sync vs async, dry-run vs execute).
evals.reference_answer: Important architectural fact to surface up-front: as of phase 2A the `af plan-exec` CLI does NOT dispatch steps to skills — the module docstring in plan_exec_cli.py explicitly says dispatch is the `subagent-driven-development` skill's job in phase 2C. `af plan-exec` is a validator + state tracker + next-node picker. The real 'execution' is driven by a skill reading `af plan-exec next` and marking nodes done via `af plan-exec mark`. Numbered flow for `af plan-exec <subcommand> <plan.yaml>`: (1) Shell invokes `af` console script → tools/python/src/af/main.py:_run(). (2) _run() calls Typer `app()` (install/research intercept does not match here). (3) Typer routes `plan-exec` to the sub-app registered by main.py's `_register()` via `app.add_typer(plan_exec_cli.app, name='plan-exec')`. (4) plan_exec_cli.py has five Typer commands: validate, list, next, mark, reset, status. Each accepts a `plan_yaml: Path` argument (Typer validates exists/readable). (5) The command calls `_load_or_die(plan_yaml)` → plan_exec.load(path). This reads from disk (filesystem I/O), yaml.safe_load parses it, then `_coerce_node` recursively converts raw dicts into typed dataclasses (ImplementNode, VerifyNode, ReviewNode, PauseNode, LoopNode with body recursion). Raises PlanParseError on malformed input. (6) Validation (validate command only): plan_exec.validate(plan, repo_root) runs 7 checks: unique ids across top-level + loop bodies; depends_on references resolve to top-level ids; cycle detection via Kahn's algorithm on in_degree/adj maps; type-specific required fields (review.reviewer must be in {spec, code_quality}; pause.prompt non-empty; loop has items XOR from, max_parallel in [1, 10], non-empty body); on_fail enum in {retry, escalate, pause}; files.create paths must NOT exist on disk; files.modify paths MUST exist on disk. Filesystem I/O at the files.* checks. Exits 1 with issues, 0 clean. (7) Toposort (list command or internally in ready_nodes): plan_exec.toposort builds in_degree/adj from depends_on, runs Kahn's alphabetically within ready set, raises ValueError on cycle. Returns List[Node]. (8) State tracking: plan_exec.StateFile.load reads `<plan>.yaml.state.json` if present (filesystem I/O); states are {node_id: pending|running|done|failed}. StateFile.mark(node_id, status) updates the map and writes back to disk (filesystem write). StateFile.ready_nodes(plan) returns toposort-ordered nodes whose own state is pending AND all depends_on are done. (9) `next` command: prints one JSON line per ready node → stdout. This is the handoff: the driving skill (subagent-driven-development, phase 2C) is expected to consume this, invoke an agent/subagent per node, then call back into `af plan-exec mark <id> done` (subprocess) to advance. No subprocess is spawned inside plan_exec itself. Forks in the path: (a) command verb — validate vs list vs next vs mark vs reset vs status pick different leaf functions; (b) reset --yes vs interactive — reset confirms via typer.confirm unless `--yes` bypasses; (c) repo_root resolution in validate — explicit flag > walk-up for .git > cwd (the _resolve_repo_root helper). Data transforms: YAML text → yaml.safe_load dict → _coerce_node dataclasses → validate error list (stringly-typed); states JSON → {id: status} dict → persisted JSON on mark. External calls: filesystem reads (plan yaml, state json), filesystem writes (state json on mark, unlink on reset). No network, no subprocess spawning from plan_exec itself — subprocesses happen one level up in the driving skill. Hook integration: skill-logger.py observes tool calls at the Claude level, logs Read of SKILL.md to ~/.claude/logs/agentfiles.jsonl and all tool calls to ~/.claude/logs/sessions/. Orthogonal to plan_exec's execution path; they meet only at the shared log pipeline.

evals.name: abstractions-hooks-system
evals.prompt: The /home/eastill/projects/agentfiles repository has a hooks system under the `hooks/` directory. Identify and explain the 3–5 core abstractions that hold the hooks system together. For each abstraction: name it, say which file(s) define it, describe its shape (data fields or interface), explain what it's FOR, and name 2–3 concrete consumers in the codebase. Don't just dump file contents — I want the conceptual spine.
evals.reference_answer: The hooks system rests on five abstractions. (1) TYPED PAYLOAD SCHEMAS — defined in hooks/hook_types.py. A set of TypedDicts (BasePayload + per-event: SessionStartPayload, UserPromptSubmitPayload, PreToolUsePayload, PostToolUsePayload, StopPayload, PermissionRequestPayload, NotificationPayload), all with `total=False` so payloads are permissive. Plus module-level helpers `parse(event, raw)` (returns {} on any error — never raises), `tool_name(payload)` (handles tool_name vs tool fallback), and `bash_command(payload)` (tool_input.command > input.command > command fallback chain). Purpose: stable, typed contract for JSON stdin; the parse() helper enforces the fail-safe exit-0 posture on bad input. The file is explicitly named `hook_types.py` not `types.py` because the hooks dir is on sys.path and a types.py would shadow the stdlib. Consumers: skill-logger.py, safety-gate.py, modes.py, notify.py — each parses stdin through hook_types.parse() first thing. (2) HOOK REGISTRATION — hooks/hooks.json. A JSON object mapping each Claude Code hook event (SessionStart, UserPromptSubmit, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, PermissionRequest, Notification) to a list of {matcher?, hooks: [{type, command, async?}]} entries. `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` is interpolated at install time. Purpose: single source of truth for what script fires on what event with what tool matcher (PreToolUse is filtered to `Bash`; PostToolUse matches `.*`). Consumers: install-hooks.sh (merges into ~/.claude/settings.json), install-gemini-hooks.sh, the Claude Code runtime. (3) HOOK SCRIPT CONVENTION — every executable under hooks/ (skill-logger.py, safety-gate.py, notify.py, modes.py, session-start) follows the same contract: shebang (`uv run --script` with inline deps for the Python ones), read JSON from stdin, call hook_types.parse(event_name, stdin), perform side effect, exit with a code (0 = allow / no-op, non-zero from a PreToolUse gate = block). Scripts prepend their own dir to sys.path so `import hook_types` resolves. Purpose: uniform, composable, single-responsibility hooks — new hooks just copy the template. Consumers: the Claude Code runtime invokes them per hooks.json; install-hooks.sh chmods them +x. (4) TWO-LAYER LOGGING PIPELINE — skill-logger.py implements and owns two distinct logs. Layer 1: session trace at `~/.claude/logs/sessions/session-<id>.jsonl` capturing every tool call (ts, session, tool, input, truncated output) — 7-day retention. Layer 2: skill invocation log at `~/.claude/logs/agentfiles.jsonl` capturing only Reads of `**/SKILL.md` (ts, skill name, parent_skill, escalated, self_loop, chain_depth) — 5 MB rolling rotation. Also maintains per-session state in `~/.claude/logs/.sessions/<id>.json` (last_skill, skills_seen, escalated flag) with 24h retention. Cleanup throttled by a marker file, runs roughly every 50 invocations if the marker is >1h old. Purpose: capture skill usage and full session traces for later introspection and catching escalation patterns (manager loaded after executor). Consumers: `af log` (tools/python/src/af/log.py), `af skill-usage`, `af learn`, `af metrics`. (5) POLICY / GATING HOOKS — safety-gate.py (PreToolUse on Bash matcher; blocks risky tool invocations by exiting non-zero) and modes.py (UserPromptSubmit; enforces active behavioral-mode rules and re-injects the mode header on every turn). Purpose: policy layer that can veto a tool call or shape every prompt, composable on top of the skill system. Consumers: the Claude Code runtime (consults them on every gated event); `af mode on/off` (tools/python/src/af/mode.py) toggles mode state that modes.py reads. Secondary glue: install-hooks.sh / install-gemini-hooks.sh merge hooks.json into user settings; hooks/tests/ covers payload parsing.

evals.name: where-to-change-skill-loading
evals.prompt: Suppose I need to change how skills are loaded in the agentfiles repo at /home/eastill/projects/agentfiles — specifically, I want to add a new source for skills (say, skills fetched from a remote URL) in addition to the existing on-disk skill loading. Walk me through exactly which files and functions I would touch to make this change, in the order I'd touch them. For each file, say WHY I'm touching it and what kind of change it needs (new function, modified existing function, new config flag, etc.). Flag any cross-cutting concerns (tests, hooks, docs, manifest entries) I would need to update.
evals.reference_answer: Skill loading in this repo is anchored on three things: manifest.toml (declarative skill registry), install.py (copies/symlinks skills into ~/.claude/skills/ at install time), and the fragment expansion in includes.py (run at skill-invocation time to inline `includes:` fragments). NOTE: there is no runtime 'skill loader' service — Claude Code itself reads ~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md on demand; agentfiles' job is to get the files there. That shapes the whole change. Ordered change list: (1) manifest.toml — the declarative source. Extend the existing `[skills.<name>]` schema to accept an optional `source` field with values `local` (default) or `remote = "https://..."`. This is the design anchor: pick the shape here first because everything else follows. Touch type: schema extension. (2) tools/python/src/af/install.py — two functions to update. (a) `_parse_manifest` — the current TOML-lite parser needs to recognize and capture the new `source` / URL fields (currently only handles tools, mcp, cli, category). Modify in place. (b) `_discover_all_skills` — currently walks `agentfiles/` for SKILL.md files locally; extend to also pull remote-declared skills into a local cache (e.g., `~/.cache/agentfiles/remote-skills/<name>/SKILL.md`) before the discovery walk, so the installer's downstream logic sees a uniform on-disk layout. Add a new helper `_fetch_remote_skill(name, url, cache_dir)` that downloads, verifies (checksum if declared), and writes to the cache. (c) Main install flow — hook the fetch step before the copy loop that installs into `~/.claude/skills/`. Touch type: one new helper, two modified functions, one new flow step. (3) tools/python/src/af/includes.py — fragment expansion walks `agentfiles/includes/`. If remote skills want to declare `includes:`, decide: either (i) disallow and document, or (ii) also fetch their include fragments into the cache. For (ii), generalize `find_repo_root` and `includes_root` to accept a list of root candidates. Small change if you go with (i); moderate if (ii). (4) tools/python/src/af/skill_find.py — the skill keyword search walks `agentfiles/` from the repo root. If remote skills should be discoverable via `af skill-find`, extend `_find_skills_root` or add a secondary root scan pointing at the cache. Touch type: small modification. (5) install.sh / install-hooks.sh — shell bootstraps. Only touch if the cache directory needs to be pre-created or if you want a `--no-remote` escape hatch. Often just docs. (6) Hook integration — hooks/skill-logger.py already logs skill reads by skill_name derived from the parent directory name of SKILL.md; if remote skills land in a different parent tree, consider whether the escalation logic (which keys on literal `manager`/`executor` names) needs source-tagging. Low-priority unless you want remote-vs-local attribution in `af skill-usage` / `af metrics`. Touch type: optional enhancement — add `source` to the logged entry schema in skill-logger.py and a migration in log.py / metrics.py. (7) Cross-cutting: TESTS under tools/python/tests/ — new tests for manifest parsing of remote entries, install.py fetch + cache behavior (mock HTTP), failure modes (unreachable URL, checksum mismatch). DOCS — update docs/reference/skills.md (and docs/skill-tree.md if present) to explain the new source type. Update README.md install instructions if the fetch changes the offline-install story. SECURITY — adding remote fetch introduces a trust boundary; document that remote URLs should be pinned by checksum or served from a trusted source. Consider adding a `--no-remote` CLI flag to `af install` for fully offline installs. CLI SURFACE — maybe a new `af install --refresh-remote` to re-fetch cached remote skills. Order of touch: manifest.toml (shape) → install.py (fetch + install plumbing) → tests → includes.py / skill_find.py (discoverability) → docs. The entry point for the change is manifest.toml — everything else is downstream of the declared shape. Note: plan_exec.py and plan_exec_cli.py do NOT need changes — they don't load skills, they just validate YAML and track state; the driving skill (subagent-driven-development) is the one that invokes skills, and it reaches them via Claude Code's built-in SKILL.md resolution, not via our code.
